sentences.text: It's just-- --when we were told that our lives were spared, every-- we-- we broke off-- we-- we were crying, we were-- we were-- [SIGHS] --singing 'God Bless the King' and-- and we were singing the national anthem and we were kissing each other and-- and-- and hugging each other.
And we-- we just couldn't believe we got home and-- and our lives had been spared, you know.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] --and sad thing was that shortly after that I remember the-- the change of government and I remember all these things are beginning to happen.
And one night, I remember in the middle of the night, the bells began to ring all over the city.
And I can feel the anxiety in my mother and father.
It was-- was it was an announcement that you could hear through the sound of the bells.
They knew what was going on.
It had to be for something really big to happen for them to be ringing all over.
And all we said in the world was all-- all over town, the king is dead.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --there had been allegations that the king was poisoned by the Germans for two reasons.
One of them is the reluctance to turn the Jews of Bulgaria to the Nazis, to turn them in and to send him to the camps.
And the other was his flat refusal to the Germans to have the Bulgarian Army fight on the Eastern Front against Russia.
Well, you have to understand something very quickly about the Bulgarian people.
If they have a monkey running Russia, they would be loyal to Russia.

Because when the Bulgarians in the 19th century rebelled against the Turks and they were about to be wiped out when the Turks brought their elite army, which was called the [TURKISH], She the Russian Tsar Nikolai, they call him the Liberator, and the have a-- anyway, he came and he helped them defeat the-- the-- the Turks.
So Bulgaria would always owe a debt of gratitude to Russian.
That was when we get all that information, not after that.
I'm listening to that as a child and they're telling us that the king was dead.
And it was-- at that time, the Jews absolutely took it real hard.
And they felt very strongly that they was a big part.
I thought it was solely, but I'm not going to get into that controversy.
But they was a big part.
Bottom line is that he did not sign the edict and he paid with his life for it, whatever controversy may be involved after that, and I really don't care.
I do know that the king, the clergy and the Bulgarian people-- a combination of all-- spared the Jews.
The Third.

Subsequently, two years ago, his son, who was in exile, King Simeon II, came to Los Angeles and the Jewish National Fund bestowed upon his father the Legion of Honor posthumously for saving the Jews of Bulgaria.

And also a forest on October 21st will be dedicated in honor of the king, the Bulgarian people, and the clergy and the intelligentsia, all these people that were so-- so very much responsible for our-- for our lives.

Now, knowing all this, seeing the-- the-- and hearing that the Jews of [INAUDIBLE] and Macedonia were not spared and then all of a sudden, our lives were spared and then all of a sudden we're on a train going to Israel, we had to say with each other, you know what?
We were children.
Come on, we are 14-- I was 14 years old.
But luckily, that trend could have been going in a different direction.
So that's very--
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --those are some of the feelings that we have with us while we're going to-- to Palestine, of course- jubilation and joy and-- and anticipation of a new life.
But you can't divorce yourself and forget something which sheer miracle really had happened, that our lives were spared.
And it's said that the communists squelched it and there is no place on earth that 50,000 Jews were severed in their entirety during the Nazi occupation.
There's no such thing and very few people know that.
And I hope to God that this tape will convey that to people and something will be done about it.
So, well, going to Israel on the train was-- was a real experience.
I mean, away from the parents.
I tell you, we were not overcome with sadness.
No, no.
Don't forget we were kind of tough already by then.
We had lived under the Nazis, we living under the most adverse conditions.
We've seen it all-- well, not all but we've seen a lot.
So going to-- to-- to Palestine and looking forward to become part of a-- of a community there and a vital part of that community was exciting.
We knew what was expected there.
We knew that we'd be in a kibbutz, we knew that we'd work in the farms, we'd be-- and we'd be studying in the afternoon.
So in the train, the-- the mood was not a mood of depression.
It was a mood of jubilation, but it was also a mood of-- of gratitude, saying, hey, again, to reiterate that, this train could have been going in a different direction.
Whoa.
It was beautiful.
I mean-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --we arrived there, it was in the winter.
And they received us there with open arms.
We were 32, 34 children and young lads full of enthusiasm, full of life.
Most of us spoke Hebrew because we went to Hebrew school and those did not very quickly learned.

So in a way, I have a little bit of resentment that I did not study in a French college just like the rest of my cousins, but in the way I feel also grateful that-- that-- that-- that Zionism and that Jewishness was embedded so deep into me from day one.
They were wonderful there.
They called us the Bulgarians.
And we used to dance the Horah all kinds of nights.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We worked a half a day and half a day we studied.
We had a wonderful teacher, a wonderful teacher.
His name-- just he passed away.
I visited him two years ago.
We had a reunion, 50-year reunion, two years ago in Geva.
And also last month we had the 50th reunion of the kibbutz that we founded.
Now, we had two years in Geva.
After that, we got together with several other groups, children primarily from Bulgaria, and those three groups, we formed a kibbutz in the desert.
It was like a paramilitary in 1946, if you can imagine.
We were 16 years old.

But before that, I was also in a British detention camp because our-- we were in the underground and in the underground, as part of the underground, although I did not participate personally into that, but groups of our kibbutz went and they blew up some-- some bridges in protest of the British because the British had instituted a policy of stopping the refugees from the camps, the remnants of the Holocaust, to come to Israel.

And so in order for us to-- to show our total disagreement, that's what they did, whereby the more-- [SIGHS] --more ultra rights, the-- the [INAUDIBLE] and the [INAUDIBLE], they went into-- didn't stop even at killing soldiers.
And I must tell you, there was a time in Israel where it was very, very close to jumping the fence and going over to them because I was enraged that our brothers could not come to Israel.
But I stayed in the kibbutz.
All of them.
Well, in our group there were 42.
Oh, yes.
Off the top of my head, a couple of thousand maybe.
I'm really not sure, but definitely in the hundreds, 1,800 to 2,000--
More or less.
From Bulgaria.
Yes, yes.
And they were all over.
They were-- they were dispersed all over the kibbutzim.
And I have to tell you that the Bulgarian [INAUDIBLE] was one of the most industrious [INAUDIBLE] that came to Israel.
They were self-sufficient, they were self-reliant, they immediately integrated into the mainstream.
And there was a-- there was a wonderful group of people.
We don't have stars.
We don't have a Victor Borge, we don't have a Spielberg that can really tell the whole world what happened to us.
Now, very quickly to deviate from that, Victor Borge took it upon himself as a Dane to tell the whole world that the Danish people-- and they were wonderful, no question about it.
Most of the Jews were saved over there.
There were 7,000 Jews and I believe 5,000 of them were saved.
Fantastic, great.
But nobody knows-- very few people know throughout the world that 50,000 Jews in their entirety were saved in Bulgaria.
So my personal mission is to make sure that people begin to know about that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Life was beautiful there, it really was.
We worked a half a day--
[SIGHS] Well, it was like five hours and then three or three and a half hours we studied in the afternoon.
I love working with animals, so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --that we would go and we'd work in the fields.
We would harvest, we would sow.
We would harvest potatoes, we would harvest [INAUDIBLE], which was called.
It was the food that we would get for the cows.
And that would be in the carriage and carrying the stuff back to the-- to the [INAUDIBLE], the places where the cows were housed, you know.
I can't think of the word in English right now.
So-- so primarily with animals.
Also loved riding horses.
So I always used to go there and the-- the keeper of the animals, his name was an old gentleman, [?
Ilmi. ?] He was a vegetarian and he loved animals.
He absolutely kind of was my mentor because he knew how much I loved animals.
So there was a Arabian thoroughbred that was pregnant.
So I would come every afternoon, ride it all over, trot with it every day, until you could see-- we'd feel that he was getting bigger and bigger.
Because he felt that by horses exercising all the time, it would give birth easier, which it did.
But I did not witness.
I wanted to witness it, but I didn't.
But primarily-- but we were on tractors, we were in the fields, we were in the-- in the-- in anything that they had.
Citrus, they had citrus there, they would pack citruses, they would ship them out to the cities.
So anyplace-- there were so many different branches and every one of us would go and specialize in a different branch.
So like when we had the citrus, they went to the citrus.
When they went to the fruit branch, they went to the fruit branch.
There were also people that were just building, so I worked with them also in building, worked with some new construction there, whatever.
And-- but they were wonderful to us there, wonderful to the point that they wanted us to remain after our two years to become members of the kibbutz.
Well, I was one of those gung hos that says, you know, you taught us so well, we want to establish our own to show you that we're worthy of your tutelage.
And so we went and we founded our own, which the name of our kibbutz was called [INAUDIBLE], which means lights.
And it was in the Negev.

There were the first 11 strategic points that the Jewish National Fund had the wisdom and the Ben-Gurion and all the officials at that time to establish because Ben-Gurion always felt that the Negev would be a big part of the developing of the Jews.
So that's what we did then.
But life overall was just wonderful there, you know, except that-- and they were very much into defense and very much in the underground.
So the minute we began, I was 14, 14 and a half already.
We also-- they began to train us in weaponry.
They began to train us in anything military so we would know how to defend ourselves.
So they formed this outfit with us and the day every so often, we would go on training.
And we continued after that.
And that's when we became part of the underground after that.
But overall, the time in Geva, the two years, I could say that they were the most wonderful-- two of the most wonderful years I spent in my life.
Yes.
[DRINKS] I remember till today the name of the street in [INAUDIBLE] and I would drive them in Bulgarian.

And then when we established our own kibbutz, though, and during the Israeli War of Independence, because we were besieged in the Negev for six months and they didn't receive any letters from me, they thought I was gone.
Yes.

Because a lot of the Bulgarian, some that came later, immediately was conscripted into the army and they really were thrown in the-- on the road to Jerusalem and they fought there and a lot of them lost their lives there.
And most of my friends that I lost during the war, I lost them on-- in the fight of Jerusalem, not so much in the Negev as much as I lost a lot of my friends on the road to Jerusalem.
Well, I wouldn't say that we were in the military.
We were in the paramilitary, you know.
We were in the underground and we were part of the Hagana.
And then when we had our kibbutz in the desert, we were all under the command of the Palmach, which was the elite force of the Hagana.
So we were part of it.
And we trained with them and we grew up with them.
And one of our main things in that time was to guard the water pipe that they had put from Nir Am down to all the settlements so we can get water.
And the Arabs enjoyed blowing it up at times, so we-- our-- one of the main duty was to watch over that water pipe.
And of course, there were other duties after that.
And during the war, we left Geva.
We established [INAUDIBLE].
And it was-- it was a tough time.
I would say there was another miracle.
If you can think about those days in Israel, there were only 600,000 Jews.

And when the War of Independence broke out and Ben-Gurion announced that we are a state now and we were all related and we're in the kibbutz and all of a sudden, we were attacked by all these Arab armies.
And we were in an outpost there that we were sent to guard it.
And the British-- I mean the-- the Egyptians were passing through with their armor.
They could have overrun us.
And our settlement was bombed by the-- by the Egyptians.
And they were on top of a hill which is called Tell el'Farah.
But it was a very minor one and luckily we did not get any-- any serious damages to our kibbutz.
The only serious damage was one of the girls that came with us that night because of the explosions of the bombs died of a heart attack.
So it was a tough time, especially after we established the kibbutz.
Then we besieged by the Egyptian army.
We were totally cut off.
And the only way we could go is with small planes that we would drive.
And I was wounded slightly, no big deal.
But they sent me to a-- with another friend of mine from the kibbutz-- they sent us to a-- another big kibbutz where they had a Recovery Center.
So we spent three weeks there getting ourselves fattened up and eat well and-- and get better.
It was a great furlough at that time.
How old were you at that time?
My goodness, I was 18, 17. 1949, '48. 18.
[SIGHS] In Bulgaria, they-- they had some very tough times there.
My father was able, because of his background, to get a job working in a warehouse as a-- in charge of the warehouse and dealing primarily in merchandise.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- they were not persecuted per se, but the Jewish life was totally eliminated under the communists and that's why they were there, under the communists.
No Jewish life whatsoever.
And some of them became more pious than the pope and some of the Jewish communists became real involved in the government and they stayed there and they wanted to show how great they were.
So they really went overboard.
Because I always said that Jewishness-- Judaism and communism just don't mix.
And so I was offended when the communists would say they were Jews.
Well, they were not Jews.
They didn't practice any Jews.
They were Jews by birth, they didn't acknowledge anything.
And until today, they don't have a rabbi there today.
But the main synagogue in Bulgaria was just reopened, and I'm hoping that we will undertake Rabbi Asa and myself.

Rabbi Asa, by the way, had given an interview already to the show here that we'll-- we'll undertake to see that those synagogues are preserved because there were some very beautiful synagogues and they're not being taken care of.
To this day.

And when I was in-- in Bulgaria and approached that subject to the Shalom people, they say, we don't have enough money to keep our pensioners going, so we don't have enough money to even have anything to do with the upkeep of that.
No.
No.
[LAUGHS] We were very secular.
No.
In the kibbutz, we-- we practice every-- every holiday in-- in our own way.
I mean, we had Passover and we had Purim and we have Lag Ba'Omer.
And we held the holidays, but we did not have a synagogue.
We did get married by rabbis and we-- we-- we had all these rituals with us, but we were not religious-- not to the point.
I mean, we observed everything, but--
To a point.
To a point, yes.
Exactly.
In fact, not long ago, my friend sent me a tape because I missed singing it and my son, who is the organist and choir director of Sinai, heard it's very well done.
So it's really in Ladino singing all the ritual songs of Passover.
A lot of them were skipped.
And many of the-- many of the food dishes that we were taught as-- as-- our-- as children, they still cook quite a bit of that.
Ah.
It's like-- my favorite, it's-- it's like ratatouille but it's got beef in it and it-- that's what gives the flavor.
And they-- after that-- after you cook it, the best thing is to let it go for one day and let it go simmer and after that, like it forms a little bit of a crust and is crisp.
That's my favorite dish.
It's called ghivetch.
Then it have pastel, which is like-- oh, it's-- it's like a turnover.
But I mean, it's a large one with phyllo dough and inside filled with-- with like a hamburger meat.
But very, very tasty with all kinds of spices in it.
And the third one is the-- it's called [INAUDIBLE], which were stuffed bell peppers with rice and meat.
And on and on and on and on.
I can go there.
So when I go to Israel, I always gain too much weight.
But my sister-in-law that learned from my saintly mother how to cook, she's a marvelous cook and she does that.
But they-- they do keep the tradition.
My brother, they eat all the time the same thing.
So I'm very happy--
I had not seen my parents in five years.
And it was just a year after the war.
And I remember going by bus and the bus was so full because everybody was going there that a few of us got on top of the bus and I got soaked wet because it rained.
And the-- their-- their boat was docked in Haifa and I'm just waiting to see, and finally they-- I spot my father and then I saw but my mother, God rest her soul.
And I said, where is my brother?
And all of a sudden, there comes my brother, pops up.
And-- and I could spot he was white as a ghost.
I mean, being five days about that ship and they were packed like sardines there.
But it was just one incredible emotion to see them and-- and-- and of course, my mother just wouldn't let go once it started.
My father was a little bit more reserved, [LAUGHS] you know.
But my mother just wouldn't let go, you know.
And my father saw me, but then my kid brother was hanging onto me [LAUGHTER] by-- for dear life.
And after that, they went to it's called Ma'Bara, which was like a place of transit.
And from there, we took them into a dwelling.
And my father, at that time of all things, he turned farmer.
And we had the place right near a small-- small like a backyard and on his own he developed that backyard and raised bananas.
It blew our mind.
I mean, that was our father, the husband, this is-- but no, he's in Israel.
He's going to be a farmer.
He became a farmer.
So-- and my mother was a housekeeper, you know.
And then I took my brother immediately under me and I made sure that he went to the best schools.
Like I said before, he just did not like to study.
He just wanted to do things with his hands, so we put him into a [INAUDIBLE], a very, very excellent trade school and he became a craftsman.
He was-- well, he and his partner which graduated the same school, they've been partners for over 30 years.
There's two of them.
What do they do, they do with their hands.
So they build-- if you move into a house in Israel, it's only the walls.
So they come in there and the architect draws the plan and according that plan, they build everything custom made.
And we were just there a few weeks ago and my wife went to see some of the work that my brother does.
And we're about to remodel our house.
She says, why can't you come and do that for ourselves?
But that's what he does.
Was there difficulty in assimilating to their new life?
Surprising enough, no.
No.
They did extremely well with that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother was-- in Hebrew, they have a special word, it's called eshit chayil, woman of valor.
Whatever circumstances, she was always the one to say that the-- the glass half full or half empty, it was always full.
So they did the best they could with whatever they have.
She was just great.
And still continued-- she picked up Hebrew so quickly.
I mean, she knew some basic one.
But she picked up the literary Hebrew just as she picked up the literary English here.
So whatever she was there, she always turned everything for the best for us.
So she studied and she read and she continued.
And they cooked and they worked and they worked in the most difficult condition, never complaining.
I never heard my parents complain.
Thank God we're in Israel.
Thank god we're home.
How long did your family stay in Israel?
Quite a while.
I mean, we brought him here in 1962.
So from-- about 13 years they stayed in Israel.
No.
No.
No, no.
I was in the kibbutz and I-- right next door to us, there was another kibbutz.
And there were Americans there and my-- the mother of my children.
